Core_Claim: Hospital pressure is more visible, urgent and politically salient than upstream access failure.
Key_Public_Evidence_or_Theory: Health NZ role descriptions; health target and hospital-pressure documents; political salience theory
Assumptions_to_Test: Degree of budget crowd-out requires empirical testing
Falsification_Risk: If hospital growth is fully explained by demographics/workforce independent of upstream access, mechanism weakens.

Core_Claim: Commissioning and delivery responsibilities create asymmetric operational pressure across hospital and primary/community sectors.
Key_Public_Evidence_or_Theory: Health NZ Crown entity role; 2020 Health and Disability System Review
Assumptions_to_Test: Extent to which operational baselines constrain primary care access funding requires budget analysis
Falsification_Risk: If appropriations and internal controls fully protect primary care growth, mechanism weakens.

Core_Claim: Dominant capitation may under-reward additional clinically necessary contacts once a patient is enrolled.
Key_Public_Evidence_or_Theory: Ministry capitation reweighting material; Sapere briefing; microeconomic marginal-cost logic
Assumptions_to_Test: Need evidence of closed books, wait times, co-payment changes and practice viability under capitation
Falsification_Risk: If capitation plus co-payment/programme funding already funds marginal supply adequately, mechanism weakens.

Core_Claim: Patients respond to constrained access by waiting, paying, delaying, using telehealth, ambulance or ED.
Key_Public_Evidence_or_Theory: Primary care access target material; ED demand literature to be reviewed
Assumptions_to_Test: Pathway probabilities need empirical calibration by deprivation/rurality/age
Falsification_Risk: If unmet primary care need rarely converts to ED/hospital use, downstream mechanism weakens.

Core_Claim: PHO functions may add value, but payment intermediation may add opacity, transaction cost or entry barriers.
Key_Public_Evidence_or_Theory: 2020 Health and Disability System Review; 2025 PHO finance briefing; OIA trail
Assumptions_to_Test: Need direct provider and PHO stakeholder validation plus financial/contract analysis
Falsification_Risk: If PHO intermediation measurably reduces transaction cost and improves supply expansion, reform hypothesis changes.

Core_Claim: ACC activity-based payments may stabilise primary care supply and should not be assessed in isolation.
Key_Public_Evidence_or_Theory: ACC funding architecture sources to be expanded; provider revenue data needed
Assumptions_to_Test: Materiality of ACC revenue to practice viability requires data/OIA/stakeholders
Falsification_Risk: If ACC revenue is not material to practice capacity, hypothesis weakens.

Core_Claim: Ambulance can function as hospital-avoidance infrastructure, but ED conveyance may be the default unless alternatives are funded and governed.
Key_Public_Evidence_or_Theory: Health NZ Ambulance Team performance and commissioning material
Assumptions_to_Test: Need data on treat-and-refer, hear-and-treat, non-conveyance safety and ramping
Falsification_Risk: If alternatives to conveyance are unsafe, unavailable or not cost-effective, mechanism narrows.

Core_Claim: Funding rules can create artificial professional bottlenecks if narrower than legal scope and clinical governance.
Key_Public_Evidence_or_Theory: Scope-of-practice regulation; multidisciplinary primary care literature to be reviewed
Assumptions_to_Test: Need feasibility and safety review by profession/contact type
Falsification_Risk: If non-GP providers cannot expand safe capacity in key contact types, expected supply gain falls.

Core_Claim: Telehealth extends access but may weaken local in-person capacity if not integrated and if funding signals favour remote scale.
Key_Public_Evidence_or_Theory: Telehealth literature and NZ access documents to be expanded
Assumptions_to_Test: Need data on substitution, continuity, examination/procedure requirements, rural capacity
Falsification_Risk: If telehealth reliably complements rather than substitutes local capacity, risk lower.

Core_Claim: Co-payments can signal demand and allow consumer choice, but can also deter necessary care and worsen equity.
Key_Public_Evidence_or_Theory: NZ primary care access and affordability literature to be reviewed
Assumptions_to_Test: Need price-elasticity estimates by patient group and contact type
Falsification_Risk: If co-payment protections eliminate demand signalling or equity harm remains high, design needs revision.

Core_Claim: Top-tier reporting changes management attention; upstream access needs hospital-equivalent visibility.
Key_Public_Evidence_or_Theory: NZ health targets; ambulance reporting; National Primary Care Dataset target documents
Assumptions_to_Test: Need evidence that target elevation changes budget/management behaviour
Falsification_Risk: If KPIs create gaming or reporting burden without capacity improvement, KPI strategy weakens.

Core_Claim: Direct benefits do not replace kaupapa Māori, Pacific, locality, outreach and trust functions.
Key_Public_Evidence_or_Theory: Waitangi Tribunal Hauora; Pae Ora obligations; Māori/Pacific provider evidence to be reviewed
Assumptions_to_Test: Requires independent Māori/Pacific review and equity impact analysis
Falsification_Risk: If direct claims architecture worsens equity or undermines trusted providers, policy unacceptable.

Core_Claim: The same architecture can be framed as pro-market, anti-PHO, anti-GP, pro-patient or anti-equity depending on actor incentives.
Key_Public_Evidence_or_Theory: Political economy theory; sector submissions to be reviewed
Assumptions_to_Test: Need stakeholder mapping and communication testing
Falsification_Risk: If framing cannot be made neutral/non-partisan, policy path narrows.

Core_Claim: Unmet upstream need remains less fundable if not observed and linked to downstream hospital outcomes.
Key_Public_Evidence_or_Theory: National Primary Care Dataset documents; ambulance reporting; hospital data sources
Assumptions_to_Test: Need linked data strategy and data governance
Falsification_Risk: If data cannot link upstream access to hospital outcomes, empirical proof remains limited.
